Going into 2026, and we still don’t have basic features that every other CGM app offers:

No Apple Watch compilation support. Only a quick launch icon. 

No Lock screen support
No widget support 
No Apple Health support

No ability to adjust the readings

No ability to turn off the critical low alerts. I always dip low to around 50 at night at different times. This is known by my doctors. So I get woke up 4-5 times every single night. 

Seriously, it’s almost 2026, these are basic features at this point that you still don’t offer.

reviews.title: for recalls, burden the app not the patient
reviews.body:
26 march 2026: i received a message from my pharmacy saying that my abbott labs cgm sensor might be subject to recall.  

i had to dig around the app, transcribe the serial number into a text field of a web app, but wait! 
the serial number it doesn't start with T, there are no uppercase O so if you think it's capital O, it's really a zero. 

that is *horrible* user exprience (UX)

it is 2026, so apps most likely are on a portable device with some kind of internet access, 3g at least, probably wifi to broadband.

change request: 

the app - not the patient - should submit the serial number to an abbott web property  & tell the patient that the sensor is not subject to recall so keep using it, or stop using it & that a new sensor is in the mail, call xxx-yyy-zzzz if you don't receive in a week.

reviews.title: FIX THE ALARMS!!
reviews.body:
I love the sensor, it's a lot better than the competition and it's thinner and actually stays on without any of the ungodly sticky mess that one of the others has. BUT - I left Libreview years ago for one reason, only to come back and the SAME reason persists!! The alarms go on and on and on!!

Want to silence them? You can! Only, you can't... they STILL hassle your phone constantly. Even on the hour, "reminding" you that you've got your alarms silenced!! Plus the sounds the alarms makes CANNOT be changed. Supposedly, they can be "lowered" in tone but then you cannot hear your phone ring or anything - it's tied to your phone's sound bar. Or so it says. When my sugars go low, I just get CONSTANT blaring, jagged SCREAMS - even if I've got the app open! 

Did you take something sweet, you're waiting for your sugars to get higher and you just want your alarms to stop? Try closing your app! It'll SCREAM at you too! Out of frustration I've just turned off my phone altogether!! It's stressful enough when you're trying to deal with low blood sugar. PLEASE FIX THIS!

reviews.title: Improvement desperately needed
reviews.body: When the sugar goes low, alarm is triggered, and triggered only once.  It should trigger, then go to sleep for X minutes (5), and if sugar isn’t changing its course it should continue to beep every 5 minutes or so.  If we don’t act the first time and get distracted (old people do tend to forget!), then it keeps on going low and lower without further warning.  DO NOT JUST WARN ONCE, KEEP ON ALERTING TILL THE CONDITION OS GONE OTHERWISE WE MAY ACTUALLY GO INTO COMMA WITHOUT FURTHER REMINDERS.   Also, there should be a way we could OPT (not mandatory) to calibrate the reading to match our manual testers so the bi-weekly sensor displays more accurate values.   Get users who opt for this to take 3 readings an hour apart from each other and execute 3 manual measurements with a manual instrument then get the unit to match the data.  Sensors are quite often 20-25 units off and we can’t do anything about that.

reviews.title: Awesome Solution
reviews.body:
As someone who has been diabetic for more than 20 years and is insulin dependent, I struggled for a long time to keep my glucose levels under control. That all changed once I started using the FreeStyle Libre sensor. Since then, I’ve been able to keep my A1C below 6.5, which is something I wasn’t able to consistently achieve before.

One of the features I appreciate the most is the sensor alarm. It has alerted me multiple times in the middle of the night when my glucose level dropped below 55 (hypoglycemia), which is incredibly reassuring and greatly appreciated.

Abbott’s customer support has been fantastic. I have had multiple sensor failures over the last couple of years, primarily due to falling off, and Abbott would send me out a replacement sensor.
 
I  highly recommend the FreeStyle Libre for anyone looking to better control their diabetes.

Thank you Abbott for providing this awesome solution.

reviews.title: Entering Up and Down on Screen is Annoying
reviews.body:
When I want to enter Notes I have to go to top of my screen and hit the + sign @ upper right then need to go to bottom of screen to hit the Notes icon. Then go back up to Comments to start typing. Then go back to lower screen and hit Done. Then back up to the top of screen and hit Save @ upper right. Why do I have to hit Done and Save? It’s redundant. This version is Not User Friendly! Techs need to place all the buttons either at top or bottom of screen. I’d suggest Bottom since that’s where the keyboard is on phones. And just as Important…Please also keep in mind a lot of diabetics have vision and neuropathy issues. Soft grey colors and tiny print are hard to see. I’m suggesting next version you use bright colors and possibly have a magnifying capability, maybe that can follow the cursor? 
I do like the Insights and the AI Assist where we can take photos of foods to determine pros and cons before we even eat it!

reviews.title: Alert overkill
reviews.body:
I got this for my mother to be able to keep track of her readings. It was more trouble than it was worth. When her sugar would go low at night it would alert her ALL NIGHT LONG. If you’re laying on on the side where the sensor is, it can give lower readings. Somehow they think the alert going off during the night and the consumer not sleeping is healthy. I eventually switched to only her left side since she didn’t sleep on her left as much, that was a little better. We’ve had issues with it staying in. For $15 a pop with insurance and wasting at least three because the sensor kept coming out and the constant alerts I decided not to renew anymore. It’s a great idea, wish it was less nosey and not so buggy.

To add to this review, if you turn off the alerts it doesn’t work. Having the alerts off she doesn’t get readings and I don’t get readings. Not sure what they gain by forcing you to have the annoying inaccurate alerts turned on. The doctor even says the finger stick is more accurate than the sensor. So why stop working if the alerts are turned off? What do they care? She knows how to look at the app and see her sugar, she doesn’t need to be needlessly reminded.

reviews.title: Excellent Devices Overall
reviews.body:
Started off with the Libre 2 years ago, but found it just wasn’t practical with daily activities (constantly knocking it off).

While I’m a non-insulin dependent “borderline” diabetic with an A1C that hovers around 5.5 with Mounjaro, I have found that I have an extremely low tolerance for any blood sugar increase.

If I stay between 105 and 120 I’m fine, but creeping up to 150+ would cause severe fatigue.

Luckily a provision of my insurance provides devices for chronic conditions at 100% coverage, so I decided to get a Libre again to log food, glucose levels and fatigue.

I have to say that the 3 + is a huge step forward in ease of use as well as being far more unobtrusive in my daily life. So for what I need it for, it has been working great.

As others have said though, an ability to display a widget and other “at a glance” data would be nice.

Also, while this isn’t a knock on the Libre 3 +, I feel I should point out that the current voucher for a 14 day device same, while showing a date in 2026, returns an error to pharmacies saying it expired in 2025. I feel this is something that should be rectified as it could easily be losing people who could greatly benefit from the device but would like to try it out before committing to any potential copays or full cash price.

The app/sensor pairs seem to be a bit subjective as I have gotten minor to moderate differences after changing sensors after 15 days of use. Not just the instantaneous readings but also the patterns of the sensor readings during the day. I don’t know how (or if) to react to alerts yet but I will be talking to my doctor for a follow up after my initial introduction.

reviews.author: Sensor loss

reviews.title: False Critical Alerts
reviews.body:
Abbot needs to build some histeresis into their algorithms for generating the low glucose critical alerts or copy what Dexcom is doing in their app to allow better adjusting and muting the alert times. A 6hr mute is in the Libre app is an improvement over earlier software versions.  However, there should be a silent mode or way of adjusting the critical alert tone. 

Fundamentally, if the sensors were more accurate it wouldn’t be an issue. I’ve use Dexcom G7 for years and have only had one low glucose, and it was real. 

The abbot sensors, I’ve use Libre 2 and Libre 3 plus, have no calibration feature, and still yield MANY false alerts and that is a big problem.

reviews.title: Needs Past 24 Hours Time In Range & Average Glucose Features Back

Those were so great and useful in the old Libre 2 app so that we could see how we were doing over a 24 hour period and perhaps adjust accordingly, instead of the 7, 14, 30 days windows under Insights in the current Libre version. Please bring those Past 24 Hours features back and make Libre even better! I’d also mention that the simplicity of the look of the old Logbook in Libre 2 app following simple listings of Glucose readings over time was also far more superior than the current look. Thanks.

reviews.title: Not sensing?
reviews.body:
Updated: Got a response from store that I needed to contact customer support and waited for new units. So finally I used a 3rd sensor they sent me. Turns out that the company doesn’t support iPhone 17 and the latest iOS. It only works with iPhone 16 and iOS 18. Would have bee good to know this before. The customer support kept offering me a free sensor. Your other option is to ask your doctor to prescribe a SEPARATE DEVICE that reads these sensors. Yes, a separate device to carry that would read the sensors. From there you have to pay for this (or your insurance) and carry your phone and the device. 

The obvious option here is that this company is not keeping up with supporting iPhones and iOS apps to support their hardware. They need to do that because it’s a critical need product. As a ceo of a hardware software connected health tech company, you have to keep your software updated especially something so critical. The iPhone 17 has been out since September 2025. Apple constantly updates the iOS. I worry that the company can’t seem to keep up.

Original Review: 
The app seems to not be able to deal with sensor and the critical alarm keeps going off. Within the last 2 hours I am on the second sensor thinking perhaps it’s the first sensor issue. It feels like it’s an app issue. The critical alarm keeps saying it’s going did. The app says it scanned and is activating the new sensor but the blue booth (turns off and back on to see if reboot) not picking up. I also restarted the phone. But it is not helping this second depart as well. Given how privy these sensor are I feel like I just wasted two $50 sensors

reviews.title: No safe way to manage false urgent alarms overnight
reviews.body:
The FreeStyle Libre 3 app has a serious usability and safety issue related to urgent low glucose alarms.

The app triggers non-disableable urgent alarms in response to known pressure-induced false lows (often called “compression lows”), which occur when the sensor is lightly pressed by a mattress, pillow, or couch. This is a well-documented CGM limitation and not true hypoglycemia.

In prior versions, users could mitigate this by closing the app overnight. The current app design removes that option entirely. The app cannot be closed, Bluetooth cannot be disconnected, and urgent alarms cannot be silenced, even when the readings are demonstrably false.

The result is repeated false emergency alarms during sleep, with no safe way to manage them. The only remaining options are turning the entire phone off or removing the sensor, both of which are unacceptable workarounds.

I am not diabetic and do not experience true low glucose events. Fingerstick testing confirms these alarms are false. This is not a medical issue, it is an app and alarm-management design problem.

When users are forced to consider buying a second phone or shutting their phone off just to sleep, that is not user error…it is a system-induced workaround behavior.

Until there is a safe way to manage urgent alarms triggered by known false positives, this app is NOT usable overnight.

reviews.title: App is not as good as Dexcom
reviews.body:
This app is not as good as Dexcom! I switched to Libre because years ago the Libre app was great. Had to switch to Dexcom for insurance reasons. After using the Dexcom app for a few years I now appreciate it! It seamlessly integrated with Apple health, it allowed me to calibrate to finger sticks. Provided multiple views in time to see glucose levels.  So many shortcomings in the Libre 3 plus app!
Also, had to scan all sensors due to an issue with sensors, found 3 were recalled. Not sure when I will see replacements.
May have to switch back to Dexcom.

Getting pressured by doctor to go on insulin now.  Got a replacement unit that fell off after a day.  App continuously beeped because there wasn’t a unit interrupting sleep.  Had to delete the app to stop the beeping so we could sleep.  Lost all the history on reinstalling.  Now, not under recall sensor is throwing false low readings waking us up at night, triggering me to falsely need to eat.  This is a truly awful product that has directly negatively impacted my health.  Can’t wait to switch off of this and onto another provider.
